---
title: "Nested Data Structures"
subtitle: "A Workshop on Multilevel Modeling"
---

# Welcome

This workshop is built around a single, uncomfortable idea: **your data is never really about your data**. Every dataset you collect is a partial, imperfect snapshot of a broader process — a process that is almost always structured, layered, and nested in ways your standard analysis ignores.

A patient's mood today is nested within their longer emotional trajectory. A consumer's rating on Airbnb is nested within their history with that host, the platform's culture, and the competitive context. A student's exam score is nested within their classroom, which is nested within their school. Even a simple between-subjects experiment — where you collect exactly one observation per person — has nesting baked in: your participants were sampled from a population, your stimuli were sampled from a population of possible stimuli, and the moment of measurement was sampled from a population of possible moments.

The field of *nested data analysis* is fundamentally about recovering the **data-generating process** (DGP) from these snapshots. The models in this workshop don't just fit your data better — they get you closer to the truth about the process that produced it.

## What This Workshop Covers

The curriculum follows a two-act structure.

**Act I — Foundations (Modules 0–3)** builds the conceptual language: how nested structure works, how coefficient meaning changes across levels, and how inference breaks. Each module adds one layer of the diagnostic toolkit.

**Act II — Applications (Modules 4–5)** applies those tools in two tracks: Module 4 for observational and longitudinal data, Module 5 for experimental and nested trial designs. Both tracks ask the same question — *can we recover a credible causal estimate?* — and answer it with context-specific tools.

The unifying question throughout is: *What assumption is standard regression making here, and what happens when it's wrong?*

::: {.grid}

::: {.g-col-12}
### Module 0: All Data is Nested
The conceptual foundation. Why the independence assumption is almost always violated, how to measure how badly, and how to think about your data as a snapshot of a larger process.
:::

::: {.g-col-12}
### Module 1: The Mixed-Effects Framework
The core toolkit: fixed effects, random intercepts, random slopes, three-level structures, and crossed random effects. With a deep focus on the assumptions each model makes and how to check them.
:::

::: {.g-col-12}
### Module 2: Centering & Between/Within Effects
One of the most consequential (and least taught) decisions in multilevel analysis: what does your predictor mean? Grand-mean centering versus cluster-mean centering, the ecological fallacy, and Simpson's Paradox.
:::

::: {.g-col-12}
### Module 3: Inferential Threats & Identification
When your predictor is not yet an independent variable: omitted variable bias, selection, reverse causality, collider bias, and experiment-specific threats. The module maps inferential threats and introduces the identification logic behind each recovery strategy — with full implementations in Modules 4 and 5.
:::

::: {.g-col-12}
### Module 4: Longitudinal Data & Change
The observational application track. Time creates both threats (time-varying confounding, attrition, staggered adoption) and leverage (within-unit variation, policy timing, natural experiments). Module 4 covers the full arc: understanding time structure, modeling trajectories, handling non-Gaussian and event outcomes, missing data, and applied causal identification tools — DiD, ITS, panel IV, and time-varying propensity scores.
:::

::: {.g-col-12}
### Module 5: Experimental Designs with Nesting
The experimental application track. Module 5 covers the full arc from clean trial design to messy real-world implementations: crossed random effects, partially nested designs, maximal vs. parsimonious random effect structures, multi-site experiments, power analysis, and — in Parts 7–9 — applied identification tools for imperfect randomization (LATE/IV, DiD, IPW for attrition, PSM within treatment arms).
:::

:::

## How to Use This Workshop

Each section pairs **conceptual explanation** with **R tutorials** and — wherever possible — both an experimental example and a secondary/observational data example. All code is shown using the toggle button at the top right of each page.

This workshop assumes you are comfortable with basic regression and have used R before. No prior exposure to multilevel modeling is assumed.

## Key Premise

> **Science combines observable data with assumptions to infer latent or general processes.** Research errors frequently stem from unexamined assumptions about the structure of that data. The goal of nested data models is not just to "control for clustering" — it is to recapture the data-generating process.

The models here are tools for thinking. Understanding their assumptions — and when those assumptions fail — is the heart of this workshop.
